attributes.stem:
An 65-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with sudden and severe tearing chest pain that radiates to the scapula. He has a blood pressure of 110/75 mmHg, respiratory rate of 12/min and pulse rate of 60/min.

The following tests are requested: renal function tests, D-dimer, ECG, CXR and transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE). A diagnosis of descending aortic dissection (Stanford type B) is made based on elevated D-dimer, widened mediastinum on a chest X-ray and dissection flap on the transoesophageal echocardiogram. There are no signs of end-organ malperfusion with normal kidney function. A CT angiogram is awaited.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the next best step in management?
attributes.explanation:
Aortic dissection is an acute aortic syndrome that occurs in 3 out of 100,000 per population. It is generally categorised into two subtype classification- Stanford A and Stanford B. Stanford A is dissection involving the ascending aorta (proximal to the brachiocephalic trunk) whereas B does not. Chronic conditions such as hypertension, vasculitis with aortic involvement are common risk factors for aortic dissection. Other risk factors that can be considered are trauma due to motor vehicle collision or congenital diseases such as Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome and bicuspid aortic valve. For an uncomplicated Stanford B dissection, overall 30-day mortality rate is 10%. A complicated dissection is associated with ischaemia and manifested with renal failure, visceral ischaemia or contained rupture. Complicated dissection have 30-day mortality rate of 25%. The acute treatment of Type B dissections is strict blood pressure and pulse rate control aiming to maintain blood pressure < 120/80 and pulse rate below 60/min to decrease the sheer stress on the aortic wall and prevent further propagation of the dissection.

Watchful waiting is the correct action to take in this case. Although the patient is hypotensive, the blood pressure is quite adequate at 110/75 mmHg as there is no end-organ malperfusion. Hence the patient is considered stable. He does not need vasopressor support or IV resuscitation at this stage. Since the patient has Stanford B dissection, immediate surgery is not warranted but medical management if needed is priority.

Immediate open surgery and endovascular treatment are not performed immediately in Stanford B dissection patients. Open surgery is performed immediately on Stanford A dissection (ascending aorta) as the patient has high risk of mortality. Endovascular treatment is performed on Stanford A dissection patients who cannot undergo open surgery or Stanford B dissection patients who develop complications such as end-organ damage, persistent severe chest pain or hypotension, and expanding aneurysm or haematoma. Endovascular stenting is often recommended when the aorta is dilated beyond 55mm, when the blood pressure is difficult to control, or when there is ongoing pain.

Thrombolytic therapy should be avoided in all patients with suspected aortic dissection as it could lead to massive bleeding.
learningPoints: Medical management and watchful waiting is the first line management of patients with Stanford type B aortic dissection.
references: Erbel R, Aboyans V, Boileau C, Bossone E, Bartolomeo RD, Eggebrecht H, Evangelista A, Falk V, Frank H, Gaemperli O, Grabenwöger M, Haverich A, Iung B, Manolis AJ, Meijboom F, Nienaber CA, Roffi M, Rousseau H, Sechtem U, Sirnes PA, Allmen RS, Vrints CJ; ESC Committee for Practice Guidelines. 2014 ESC Guidelines on the diagnosis and treatment of aortic diseases: Document covering acute and chronic aortic diseases of the thoracic and abdominal aorta of the adult. The Task Force for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Aortic Diseases of the European Society of Cardiology (ESC). Eur Heart J. 2014 Nov 1;35(41):2873-926.

Fattori R, Tsai TT, Myrmel T, Evangelista A, Cooper JV, Trimarchi S, Li J, Lovato L, Kische S, Eagle KA, Isselbacher EM, Nienaber CA. Complicated acute type B dissection: is surgery still the best option?: a report from the International Registry of Acute Aortic Dissection. JACC Cardiovasc Interv. 2008 Aug;1(4):395-402.

Diercks DB, Promes SB, Schuur JD, et al. Clinical Policy: Critical Issues in the Evaluation and Management of Adult Patients With Suspected Acute Nontraumatic Thoracic Aortic Dissection. Ann Emerg Med .2015; 65(1): p.32-42.
Howard DP, Sideso E, Handa A, Rothwell PM. Incidence, risk factors, outcome and projected future burden of acute aortic dissection.. Annals of cardiothoracic surgery. 2014; 3(3): p.278-84.
Tsai TT, Nienaber CA, Eagle KA. Acute aortic syndromes. Circulation. 2005 Dec 13;112(24):3802-13.
